perception.who_is_this_for_a: Used-car buyers who want a discounted official Carfax report quickly, with a visible sample report for reassurance; dealers are also acknowledged through a dealer link and later volume pricing.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: Individual buyers who want to enter a VIN and buy immediately, with a more explicit dealer access card for businesses needing volume reports.

reasoning: Candidate B has the cleaner task-first layout: the VIN form, purchase CTA, sample link, and dealer card are all very easy to understand in the first viewport, and dealer self-selection is stronger above the fold. However, the rendered page visibly contains placeholder identity content such as [BRAND], Reg# ABC123, [Brand], Inc., and [Founder Name]. For this high-skepticism discounted Carfax use case, that is a serious trust failure and makes the page feel unfinished or potentially illegitimate. Candidate A is slightly less strong on first-viewport dealer prominence, but it feels more complete and credible: it names the site, shows trust cues before the purchase action, presents the $9.99 savings clearly, includes a real-looking sample report preview in the hero, and provides robust lower-page dealer pricing, refund, process, FAQ, and business details. Because legitimacy is the primary conversion blocker, A is the stronger perceived page overall.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Visible placeholder identity and proof details, including [BRAND], Reg# ABC123, [Brand], Inc., [Founder Name], and gray sample-report placeholders, undermine legitimacy in a trust-critical purchase flow.
